one_line_takeaway: SG-Unlearn turns membership inference into the training signal for machine unlearning via a differentiable Stackelberg game, coming closest to the retrain gold standard on MIA/KS unlearning metrics for random forgetting (at a modest test-accuracy cost) while remaining ineffective for class-wise forgetting.
key_finding: On random forgetting SG-Unlearn most closely matches the retrain gold standard on the privacy/unlearning-effectiveness metrics. On CIFAR-10, SG's MIA accuracy is 0.5202 (retrain 0.5069; nearest random guessing among all methods) and its KS statistic between forget/test loss distributions is 0.0482 vs retrain 0.0255 - roughly an order of magnitude below most baselines (FT 0.0933, GA 0.1403). On CIFAR-100 SG's MIA accuracy is 0.5412 (retrain 0.5184) while all baselines sit at 0.66-0.70, and SG's KS stat is 0.0988 vs baselines' 0.37-0.49. The gain comes with a model-utility trade-off: SG test accuracy is 0.8940 (CIFAR-10) and 0.6378 (CIFAR-100) vs retrain 0.9291 / 0.7035, a small-to-moderate degradation. SG is far cheaper than retraining (CIFAR-10 RTE 1.47 min vs 14.92 min). An ablation (alpha=1 SG-1 vs alpha=0 SG-0) shows the adversarial term both acts as a regularizer (improving test accuracy) and shrinks the Wasserstein distance between forget/test losses over epochs. For class-wise forgetting ALL methods (including SG) perform poorly on MIA metrics because forgotten-class vs test distributions are inherently distinguishable, and no method consistently wins.
core_contribution: The paper's OWN contribution is SG-Unlearn: a game-theoretic framework that recasts approximate machine unlearning as a Stackelberg game between an 'unlearner' (leader, first mover) who edits a trained model to remove a forget set, and an 'auditor' (follower) who runs a membership inference attack (MIA) to detect residual influence of the forgotten data. Rather than treating unlearning-algorithm development and MIA development as two parallel research strands (which the paper frames as the status quo in Related Work), the authors proactively fold the attacker into the unlearning objective: the unlearner minimizes retain-set loss plus alpha times the auditor's MIA utility. The technical enabler is solving the resulting bi-level optimization by differentiating THROUGH the auditor's (convex, e.g. SVM) optimization problem using the Implicit Function Theorem and Differentiable Optimization tooling (cvxpy), yielding gradient dM/dtheta_u so the whole game becomes a differentiable layer droppable into an end-to-end pipeline. This is distinct from cited work: the exact/approximate unlearning taxonomy, influence-tracking rollback methods, and the loss-function unlearning line ([2;12-16]) are Related Work, not this paper; the adversarial-regularization defense of Nasr et al. [24] is the closest prior art but targets defending MIAs (NN-based only), whereas this paper targets unlearning and supports both NN- and non-NN-based (e.g. SVM) attacks.
threat_model: The adversary is an 'auditor' with black-box query access to the deployed unlearned model theta_u (no access to training data or model parameters). The auditor constructs an auditing set from the model's outputs (e.g. instance-wise cross-entropy losses) on forget instances (labeled member=1) and held-out test instances (non-member=0), then trains a binary classifier to distinguish them; success means residual influence of the forget set persists. Important nuance for a security graph: this adversary is used as an internal DESIGN and EVALUATION oracle (a simulated worst-case auditor the unlearner best-responds to), not primarily as an external attacker being defended in deployment. The security-relevant motivation is GDPR-style right-to-be-forgotten / data-deletion verification.
limitations: Authors' stated limitations: (1) the O(n^3) matrix inversion in the IFT gradient makes the algorithm expensive and motivates more efficient differentiation; (2) only one unlearner/auditor pairing is explored (unlearner = Fine-Tune, auditor = linear-SVM MIA) - other combinations such as Random Label + NN-based MIA are left as future work. Additional limitations evident from the results: (3) class-wise forgetting is essentially unsolved by the framework - all methods fail the MIA test; (4) a persistent test-accuracy vs unlearning-effectiveness trade-off that sweeping alpha over {0.05...5} did NOT consistently resolve toward the retrain point across all metrics; (5) NN-based attacks are noted as too slow for the runtime budget (if unlearning exceeds retraining cost one would just retrain), so the demonstrated auditor is the convex SVM; (6) evaluation is limited to three image classification datasets and one architecture (ResNet-18).

quantitative_results: CIFAR-10 random forgetting MIA accuracy: SG 0.5202 vs Retrain 0.5069 (closest to 0.5 among all methods; FT 0.5405, GA 0.5504, RL 0.5217)
CIFAR-10 random forgetting KS statistic: SG 0.0482 vs Retrain 0.0255 (FT 0.0933, GA 0.1403, SCRUB 0.1038)
CIFAR-10 test accuracy: SG 0.8940 vs Retrain 0.9291 (utility trade-off)
CIFAR-10 runtime RTE: SG 1.47 min vs Retrain 14.92 min
CIFAR-100 random forgetting MIA accuracy: SG 0.5412 vs Retrain 0.5184 (baselines 0.66-0.70)
CIFAR-100 KS statistic: SG 0.0988 vs Retrain 0.0203 (baselines 0.37-0.49)
CIFAR-100 test accuracy: SG 0.6378 vs Retrain 0.7035
SVHN random forgetting MIA accuracy: SG 0.5012 vs Retrain 0.5248; W. Dist SG 0.0185 vs Retrain 0.0686; |Accf-Accte| SG 0.0016
CIFAR-10 class-wise forgetting: all methods poor on MIA (e.g. SG MIA acc 0.9814, Retrain 0.9935); no method consistently best
Forget set sizes: 10% of training set for CIFAR-10/CIFAR-100, 5% for SVHN
Per-epoch time complexity O(n^3) from IFT matrix inversion
